Self-organized criticality on complex networks
¶

Models and Numerical Methods in Physics
¶

Riccardo Grandicelli
¶

1. Introduction¶

In nature, power-laws appear everywhere. The distribution of earthquake magnitudes, of sizes of solar flares, or of wealth in a country are just a few examples of power laws. The main characteristic of power law distributions is that they are scale invariant: if you multiply the variable by a constant factor, the distribution remains identical except for a multiplicative constant factor. This means that if you "zoom" on a power law, the shape of the distribution does not change.

Two other important phenomena related to scale invariance are fractals and "1/f" noise. Fractals are self-similar geometrical structures, while "1/f" noise is a signal in which the amplitudes of frequency components follow a power-law (so it represents scale invariance in time). Both of them are very common in nature: mountain landscapes and coastal lines are example of fractal structures, while "1/f" noise have been measured in electronic devices and in the luminosity of stars.

Seeking an explanation for this common behaviour of very different systems, in 1987 Bak, Tang and Wiesenfeld [1] first talked about "self-organized criticality" (SOC). With this term they meant the ability of many dynamical systems to reach autonomously a critical state. The word "criticality" is used in analogy with the critical point at phase transitions in statistical mechanics: indeed, at the critical point the system becomes scale invariant, and power laws start to appear. However, while the reaching of a critical point in statistical mechanics requires the tuning of a parameter (such as the temperature, for example), in self-organized systems the critical point is reached as an attractor of the dynamics, without the tuning of a parameter.

In the same paper, Bak, Tang and Wiesenfeld developed a simple toy model, called "sandpile model", to show an example of SOC. The model is a cellular automaton that tries to emulate a pile of sand, in which a new grain of sand is added at each time step.

The model is structured as follows:

  • we have a 2-dimensional square grid of N x N cells
  • we assign an initial value of grains on each cell: $z(x,y) \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$
  • at each time step $i \in \mathbb{N}$, we choose a random cell $(x, y)$ and we put one grain of sand on it: $z_i(x, y) = z_{i-1}(x, y) + 1$
  • if $z_i \geq 4$ (4 is the threshold height), the cell topples: it loses grains equal to the threshold and redistributes them among its four neighbours (along the horizontal and vertical directions), giving one grain to each neighbour
  • the toppling of a node can cause another of its neighbours to topple, giving raise to a cascade process called avalanche

Through a numerical simulation it is possible to show that after a certain number of time steps, the system reaches a stationary state in which the avalanche size distribution (the avalanche size is the number of topplings at a certain time step) follows a power law:

\begin{equation} P(s) \propto s^{-\tau} \end{equation}

which signals the presence of SOC.

In their original model, Bak, Tang and Wiesenfeld only considered a 2-d square lattice. So, the first natural idea to generalize this model was to consider network topologies different from the square lattice. Many papers have been written about this topic: in this project, we want to review the main results concerning SOC on different network topologies.

The work is structured as follows: in Section 2 we revise the sandpile model on a squared lattice developed in [1], while in Section 3 we briefly present the mean field treatment used to obtain analytically the power law exponent of the avalanche size distribution. Then in Section 4 we study the sandpile model on two important network structures: random networks and scale-free networks. Finally, in Section 5 we analyze the sandpile model on networks composed by more than one layer (the so-called multiplex networks), and see how the results change when the different layers interact with each other.

For the simulations, we will use two main Python packages. The first one is networkx, the most famous Python package to build networks. The other one, developed by me, is called SandNet: it allows to build a sandpile model over an arbitrary network structure. The instructions for the installation of the two packages are in the README.md file of the GitHub repository of this project.

In [1]:
import networkx as nx
import SandNet
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm

2. Sandpile on a 2d square lattice¶

We first begin by studying the original sandpile model developed by Bak, Tang and Wiesenfeld (1987) [1]. We will use a 100 x 100 grid with open boundaries, with a fixed threshold of 4 for each node, and a random initial number of grains for each node at the beginning. We create the model using the SandNet.Model object, and evolve it for 30000 time steps by adding new grains in random positions.

In [2]:
original_model = SandNet.Model(N = 100, initial_grains='random', seed = 42)
#the threshold of 4 and the lattice topology are default parameters, so we don't need to specify them

original_model.evolve(30000, seed = 42)
#calculates also the avalanche area, see next sections

Then we can plot the avalanche size distribution, and fit it to a power law:

In [3]:
figure_original = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(original_model, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density", 
                                     title = "Avalanche size distribution (square grid)", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent, figure_original = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(original_model, figure = figure_original, color = "red", linestyle = "--",
                                        label = "fit avalanche sizes")
print("Avalanche size exponent: " + str(exponent))

Avalanche size exponent: 0.2891527249266174

As we can read in the SandNet documentation, the exponent returned by the plot_avalanche_size function is lowered by 1 with respect to the exponent in (1). This happens because the form of the function used for the fit is the Pareto distribution:

\begin{equation} P(x) = \frac{b}{x^{b+1}} \tag{2} \end{equation}

and the parameter returned by the plot_avalanche_size function is $b$, while comparing (2) with (1) we see that the exponent $\tau$ is given by $b+1$.

So, we can see that the system organizes itself into a critical state, with a distribution of avalanche sizes well fitted by a power law of the form (1) with the exponent $\tau \approx 1.29$. In the next section we will see an analytical derivation of this exponent, and we will compare the value obtained through simulations with the one we will obtain theoretically.

It is worth noting that, when one of the boundaries of the grid topples, it loses four grains, but it only has three (if it is on a side of the grid) or two (if it is a vertex of the grid) neighbours. Since the system has open boundaries, one or two grains are lost after a toppling in a boundary position. If we didn't have dissipation of grains, the total number of grains in the system would always grow by 1 at each time step, until we would reach a point in which the system is saturated and it remains locked into an avalanche of infinite size. So, having open boundaries is essential to have a self-organized critical state.

3. Mean field treatment of sandpile model¶

The simplest approach to describe SOC theoretically is the mean field theory, which provides an approximation of the exponent for avalanche size distribution.

Each avalanche event in the sandpile model can be represented as a tree: the originator of the tree is the cell of the grid where the avalanche started, while the following cells involved in the avalanche are the descendants in the tree. The structure of the tree has a close relation with the size of the avalanche, since this one is given by the number of nodes in the tree, excluding the boundaries.

Since the avalanche can be related with a tree, the mean field treatment describes an avalanche process using the branching process: at each time step the cell which receives the grain has a probability $p$ to topple and generate a number of descendants in the tree equal to the number of its neighbours, and a probability $1-p$ to stop the avalanche without toppling. If the avalanche does not stop, we repeat the same procedure for all the neighbours of the starting cell, and go on until the avalanche stops. It is possible to show that below a certain critical value $p_c$ of the parameter $p$, we only have finite size avalanches, while above this value we have a non-zero probability of having an infinite avalanche. So, for $p=p_c$ we have the critical state characterized by power law distributions.

This model, however, does not take into account the existence of boundaries in the system, and as we said in the previous paragraph, imposing open boundary conditions is essential to have SOC. This problem is solved in Zapperi et al. (1995) [2], where they introduce a limit N to the number of descendants the avalanche tree can have. If this limit is reached, then the parameter $p$ will decrease at the next time step to account for the lost grains of sand at the boundaries, otherwise $p$ will increase. In this way, they show that, in the limit of $N >> 1$, the value of $p$ automatically converges to the critical value $p_c$. As in SOC, then, there is no need to manually tune the parameter to reach the critical state.

We will not go into the details of the theoretical calculations, since it would require the theory of branching processes which is outside the scope of this project. The final result obtained using mean field treatment is a power law distribution for the avalanche sizes with the exponent $\tau = 1.5$.

As we can see, the exponent obtained with the mean field treatment is significantly different from the one obtained for the sandpile model on the 2d square lattice. This happens because mean field theory completely neglects spatial correlations: it assumes that different cells are completely independent. This also means, for example, that in this approximation the avalanches cannot form loops, i.e. they cannot pass two times on the same node. So, at low dimensions, the mean field theory is a rough approximation, which becomes more and more exact as the dimension of the system increases. Indeed, an increase of the dimension means an increase of the number of neighbours, making negligible the role of spatial correlations.

In the next sections, we will use the mean field exponent as reference point to interpret the results we will obtain for the sandpile model on network topologies different from the square grid.

4. Sandpile model on simplex networks¶

We now turn to the study of the sandpile model on two simple network topologies: random networks and scale-free networks. Essentially, we try to reproduce the main results obtained by Goh et al. (2003) [3].

Random networks are built starting from a set of node and then drawing links randomly until the system has a certain number of links chosen before building the network. In the limit of high number of nodes and links, the node degree distribution will then follow a Gaussian distribution.

Scale-free networks are defined as networks whose degree distribution follows a power-law:

\begin{equation} P(k) \propto k^{-\gamma} \tag{3} \end{equation}

These networks are very common in nature, typically with an exponent $2 < \gamma < 3$, and are characterized by preferential attachment (some nodes are more likely to form links than others).

In the next subsection, we will see how these kind of networks can be built.

4.1 How to build random and scale-free networks¶

There are many methods to build random networks and scale-free networks. In this project, we will use the method developed in Goh et al. (2001) [4], which is also the one employed in [3]. This method depends on three parameters ($N$, $m$ and $\alpha$) that must be chosen at the beginning, and can be described as follows:

  1. Start from $N$ nodes in the system, labelled by an index $i = 1,...,N$
  2. Assign the weight $w_i = i^{-\alpha}$ to each node, with $\alpha \in [0,1)$
  3. Select two different nodes $(i, j)$ with probabilities $p_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum_{k=1}^N w_k}$ and $p_j = \frac{w_j}{\sum_{k=1}^N w_k}$, and draw a link between them if it does not exist already.
  4. Repeat step 3 until there are $mN$ nodes in the network, where $2m$ is the mean degree of the network

It can be proven that this method provides a network whose degree distribution follows a power law of the form (2) with an exponent $\gamma = 1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}$. In this way we can obtain scale-free networks with exponents in the range $2 < \gamma < \infty$.

It is easy to see that a random network can be built using this same approach, by just putting $\alpha = 0$ (so $\gamma = \infty$): indeed, in this case all nodes have the same weight, so the probability to have a link between two nodes is the same for each couple of nodes.

In order to study the sandpile model on a network structure built with this method, however, we need to solve a small issue. Indeed, the network structure built with this algorithm will likely have some nodes with no links at all, so without neighbours. While evolving the sandpile model, these nodes are completely useless, because they cannot topple and give grains to other nodes. For this reason, we modify the method developed in [4] by forcing each node to have one link with another node (chosen randomly according to the weights defined in the second point of the algorithm). After having created one link for each node, the remaining links are chosen according to the already described algorithm.

We define now a function to build networks according to this method, which we will use many times later in this project.

In [4]:
def build_network(n: int, m: int, alpha: float, seed : int = None) -> nx.Graph:
    G = nx.Graph()
    G.add_nodes_from(range(n))

    weights = (np.arange(1, n + 1)).astype(float)**(-alpha)
    probs = weights / weights.sum()

    np.random.seed(seed)

    for i in range(n):
        #for each node, link it with another node, chosen according to the weights of the nodes
        while True:
            #a node cannot be linked to itself, so the random generation is repeated until
            #a different node is found
            first_link = np.random.choice(n, p = probs)
            if(first_link != i):
                break
        G.add_edge(i, first_link)

    #now add edges until there are m * n total links
    while G.number_of_edges() < (m*n):
        u, v = np.random.choice(
            n,
            size=2,
            replace=False,
            p=probs
        )
        
        G.add_edge(u, v)

    return G

4.2 Sandpile model on random networks¶

We simulate the evolution of a sandpile model on a random network, where the threshold of each node is equal to its degree. In this way, however, the threshold of each node is always equal to the number of its neighbours, so we never lose grains during the evolution. In practice, we don't have open boundaries like in the sandpile model on a 2-dimensional lattice. Since, as we said in Section 2, this is a fundamental condition to have SOC, we have to implement a way to lose some grains during the evolution.

So, we introduce a certain probability that a grain is lost during a toppling: when the node that is toppling redistributes its grains to the neighbours, for each grain there is a small probability that the grain is lost instead of being given to a neighbour of the toppling node. This is the method employed in [3], and it should lead the system to a critical state.

In [3] they used a network of $N = 10^6$ nodes, with the parameter $m = 2$ and a probability to lose grains during a toppling of $f = 10^{-4}$. To speed up simulations, here we will use $N = 10^{4}$ instead, showing that the results will not change significantly. However, since the size of the network will be decreased, the probability to lose a grain must be increased to avoid too long avalanches covering the whole network. We will then use $f = 10^{-3}$.

These values of $N$, $m$ and $f$ will be used in all the networks we will build in the rest of the project. The only parameter we will change is $\alpha$, to switch between random networks and scale-free networks with different exponents.

We start then by building a random network with the function defined in the previous subsection, and we build a sandpile model over it.

In [5]:
random_graph = build_network(10000, 2, 0, seed = 42)
random_model = SandNet.Model(random_graph, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains='random', seed = 42)

We can verify that the network generated is really a random network by plotting the degree distribution:

In [6]:
degree_list = [list(random_graph.degree)[i][1] for i in range(len(list(random_graph.degree)))]
plt.hist(degree_list, bins = max(degree_list), color="green", density=True)
plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Degree distribution (random network)")
plt.savefig("./plots/random_network/degree_distribution_random_network.png")

As we can see the degree distribution has a shape similar to a Gaussian, as we would expect for a random network. Clearly it is not a perfect Gaussian, because we have not so many nodes: using a number of nodes higher by one or two orders of magnitude would have probably given a more Gaussian shape. Moreover, we forced all nodes to have at least one link, so nodes with degree 0 are completely missing, making the distribution a little bit right-skewed.

Now we can evolve the sandpile model on the remaining network for 30000 steps and plot the avalanche size distribution.

In [7]:
random_model.evolve(30000, lose_probability = 0.001, seed = 42)
In [8]:
figure_random = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(random_model, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density", 
                                     title = "Avalanche size distribution (random network)", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent, figure_random = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(random_model, figure = figure_random, color = "red", linestyle = "--",
                                        label = "fit avalanche sizes")
print("Avalanche size exponent: " + str(exponent))

Avalanche size exponent: 0.52437261898853

We see that the avalanche size distribution follows a power law with an exponent $\tau = 1.52$, which is very close to the mean field value. This difference with the exponent obtained on the square grid is due to the different structures of the two networks: while lattices are regular networks built according to a specific disposition of nodes, in random networks nodes are connected casually. So, in random networks spatial correlations are negligible, which is exactly the approximation we did while developing the mean field approximation of the sandpile model.

We can verify this difference between the square grid and the random network by looking at the avalanche area. The avalanche area measures the number of nodes that toppled during an avalanche. So, if a node topples two or more times during an avalanche, it is counted only once in the avalanche area (differently from what happens for the avalanche size). This means that the avalanche area is always lower or equal than the avalanche size, and the equality holds if and only if there where no loops during the avalanche (so no node toppled twice or more).

In [9]:
figure_original = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(original_model, figure = figure_original, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas", 
                                              title = "Avalanche size and area distribution (square grid)")
figure_random = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(random_model, figure = figure_random, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas", 
                                            title = "Avalanche size and area distribution (random network)")

figure_original.savefig("./plots/original_model/avalanche_distribution_square_grid.png")
figure_random.savefig("./plots/random_network/avalanche_distribution_random_network.png")

We can see that, both in the square grid and in the random network, the avalanche area distribution is almost identical to the avalanche size distribution, except for larger avalanches (since the avalanche area cannot be larger than the number of nodes in the network). However, it is more interesting to compare the biggest values of avalanche sizes and area for the two types of network:

In [10]:
print("Biggest avalanche size for square grid: " + str(max(original_model.get_avalanche_sizes())))
print("Biggest avalanche area for square grid: " + str(max(original_model.get_avalanche_areas())))
print("Biggest avalanche size for random network: " + str(max(random_model.get_avalanche_sizes())))
print("Biggest avalanche area for random network: " + str(max(random_model.get_avalanche_areas())))

Biggest avalanche size for square grid: 44876
Biggest avalanche area for square grid: 8411
Biggest avalanche size for random network: 29860
Biggest avalanche area for random network: 10000

While the maximum avalanche area is bigger for the random network (it covers the whole network), the maximum avalanche size is much smaller for the random network than for the square grid. This is due to spatial correlations in the square grid structure, leading to avalanches with a higher number of loops.

We still have loops in some avalanches for the random network, but this is just due to the small dimensions of our network. As shown in [3], using a bigger network the maximum value of avalanche size and area for the random network becomes almost the same, showing that loops totally disappear.

4.3 Sandpile model on scale-free networks¶

We now turn to sandpile model on scale-free networks. In [3] the authors found the avalanche size exponents both numerically and analytically. For what concerns the analytical derivation, they employed the branching process, and found an exponent

\begin{equation} \tau = \begin{cases} \frac{\gamma}{\gamma -1} & \text{if } 2<\gamma < 3 \\ \tau = \frac{3}{2} & \text{if } \gamma \geq 3 \end{cases} \tag{4} \end{equation}

In this subsection we try to verify these values through simulations.

Since we want to study the value of $\tau$ as a function of $\gamma$, we simulate the sandpile model evolution (30000 time steps, adding grains in random positions) for scale-free networks with different values of $\gamma$, and save the plot of avalanche size and area distributions for each value. We also save the plots of number of topplings for each node, but we will analyze them in the next subsection.

In order to save space and avoid redundancy, from now on some plots will not be shown in this notebook. However, they will be all saved in the plots folder of this GitHub repository, so that they can be easily visualized if needed.

In [11]:
gamma_string = ["5", "3", "2_8", "2_6", "2_4", "2_2", "2_01"]
for i, gamma in enumerate([5., 3., 2.8, 2.6, 2.4, 2.2, 2.01]):
    #build network for corresponding value of gamma
    alpha = 1/(gamma - 1)
    G = build_network(10000, 2, alpha, seed = 42)
    scale_free_model = SandNet.Model(G, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains='random', seed = 42)

    #plot degree distribution
    degree_list = np.array([list(scale_free_model.network.degree)[i][1] for i in range(len(list(scale_free_model.network.degree)))])
    plt.hist(degree_list, bins = max(degree_list), color = "green")
    plt.xlabel("Degree")
    plt.ylabel("Number of nodes")
    plt.title("Degree distribution (gamma = " + str(gamma) + ")")
    plt.savefig("./plots/scale_free_network/degree_distribution/degree_distribution_gamma_" + gamma_string[i] + ".png")
    
    #evolve and plot avalanche size distribution
    scale_free_model.evolve(30000, lose_probability = 0.001, seed = 42)
    figure_scale_free = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(scale_free_model, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density", 
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution (gamma = " + str(gamma) + ")", 
                                     label = "measured avalanche sizes")
    exponent, figure_scale_free = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(scale_free_model, figure = figure_scale_free, color = "red", linestyle = "--",
                                        label = "fit avalanche sizes")
    figure_scale_free = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(scale_free_model, figure = figure_scale_free, color = "orange", 
                                                    label = "measured avalanche areas")

    figure_scale_free.savefig("./plots/scale_free_network/avalanche_distribution/avalanche_distribution_gamma_" 
                              + gamma_string[i] + ".png")

    topplings_scale_free = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(scale_free_model, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node (gamma = " + str(gamma) + ")", cmap = "gist_rainbow",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5, norm = LogNorm())
    topplings_scale_free.savefig("./plots/scale_free_network/topplings_per_node/topplings_per_node_gamma_" + gamma_string[i] + ".png")
    
    print("Gamma = " + str(gamma) + "\nPower law exponent (minus 1) = " + str(exponent) + "\nMax node degree: " + str(max(degree_list)))
    plt.close('all')
plt.close('all')

Gamma = 5.0
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 0.5423334420829504
Max node degree: 29
Gamma = 3.0
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 0.6723147168208425
Max node degree: 127
Gamma = 2.8
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 0.7240097572035237
Max node degree: 191
Gamma = 2.6
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 0.7985199211697624
Max node degree: 337
Gamma = 2.4
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 0.9576687305493122
Max node degree: 586
Gamma = 2.2
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 1.2172492226813643
Max node degree: 1052
Gamma = 2.01
Power law exponent (minus 1) = 1.6634040976364146
Max node degree: 1992

We organize the results in a table for better visualization: $\tau_m$ is the measured avalanche size exponent in our simulations, $\tau_r$ is the measured exponent in [3] and $\tau_t$ is the exponent given by (4). The column "Max node degree", instead, indicates the degree of the node with the highest degree in the network in our simulations

$\gamma$ Max node degree $\tau_m$ $\tau_r$ $\tau_t$
5.0 29 1.54 1.52 1.50
3.0 127 1.67 1.66 1.50
2.8 191 1.72 1.69 1.56
2.6 337 1.80 1.75 1.63
2.4 586 1.96 1.89 1.71
2.2 1052 2.22 1.95 1.83
2.01 1992 2.66 2.09 2.0

We can verify that the exponents obtained through numerical simulations follow the trend given by the analytical formula (4): they remain close to the mean field value for $\gamma > 3$, then they start growing as $\gamma$ decreases. However, by looking at the avalanche size distributions we can see that as $\gamma\rightarrow 2$ the tail of the distribution starts deviating from the power law behaviour. This makes the obtained exponent less reliable, and particularly in the case of $\gamma = 2.01$ it is unclear whether the system has reached a critical state or not.

The general trend of the avalanche size distributions as $\gamma$ increases is an increase of the number of big avalanches with respect to smaller ones. An explanation to this behaviour can be given by looking at the degree distributions of scale free networks: as $\gamma$ decreases, the network tends towards a structure with a single node with a high degree connected to all (or almost all) the others, while all the other nodes have a degree close to 1. This means that each time the central node topples, it gives one grain to a high number of nodes (all its neighbours, unless some grains are lost). Since the threshold of each node is equal to its degree, all these nodes which have received a grain have a threshold close to 1, so all of them will topple. Each toppling of the central node corresponds then to an avalanche of large size. As we will see in the next subsection, the nummber of topplings of the node with higher degree is comparable to the number of topplings of all the other nodes, so large avalanches become more and more probable as the degree of the node with highest degree in the network increases.

4.4 Number of topplings per node¶

In this subsection we continue the comparison between sandpile model on square grid, random network and scale free network by analyzing the number of topplings for each node. During the evolution of the sandpile model on a network, SandNet also collects at each time step the number of times a certain node topples, for all nodes. We can then sum all the topplings for a certain node and plot them, by using the plot_topplings_per_node function of SandNet. Moreover, the nodes are separated by degree: nodes with lower degrees are on the left, while those with higher degree are on the right. In this way it is easier to see how the number of neighbours of a node influences the number of topplings.

We can see for example the plot of the number of topplings per node for a sandpile model on a square grid. We will use the model we have already evolved in Section 2.

In [12]:
toppling_original_model = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(original_model, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node (square grid)", 
                                                          cmap = "viridis", marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
toppling_original_model.savefig("./plots/original_model/topplings_per_node_square_grid")

As we can see, the square grid structure influences the number of topplings of each node: nodes with degree equal to 2 or 3 (so nodes at the boundaries) topple much less if compared with nodes of degree 44 (so central nodes). It is worth underlying that the y-axis of the plot is in a logarithmic scale for better visualization: this means that nodes corresponding to points at the bottom of the plot toppled a number of times which is one or two order of magnitudes lower than nodes corresponding to points at the top of the plot. Moreover, we can see that nodes of degree 4 form a curve in the plot, reflecting the symmetries of the regular lattice structure of the model.

We now plot the number of topplings per node for the random network and the scale free network. For the random network we will use the model already evolved in Section 4.2, while for the scale free models with different values of $\gamma$ all the plots have been already done in the previous subsection, and can be seen in the right folder.

In [13]:
toppling_random_model = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(random_model, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node (random network)", cmap = "plasma",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
toppling_random_model.savefig("./plots/random_network/topplings_per_node_random_network")

We can notice that now the situation is completely different: the number of topplings is almost the same for all nodes. This is due to the absence of a regular structure in random networks, that makes all nodes almost equivalent. We have in general a slightly higher number of topplings for lower degree nodes, but this is probably just due to the lower threshold of these nodes which makes it easier to have a toppling.

For what concerns scale free networks, even if there is a structure given by a small number of nodes with a lot of links and all the other nodes with only a few links, the number of topplings distribution does not show a clear structure. Indeed, even if the nodes with higher degree can receive grains from a high number of neighbours, they have a high threshold making their toppling more difficult. This balances their centrality in the network, and for this reason high and low degree nodes have almost the same probability to topple.

In [14]:
#delete some of the previously defined variables to save memory for the rest of the notebook
import gc
del alpha, degree_list, exponent, figure_original, figure_random, figure_scale_free, gamma, gamma_string, i, original_model, random_graph
del random_model, scale_free_model, G, toppling_original_model, toppling_random_model, topplings_scale_free
garbage_collector = gc.collect()

5. Sandpile model on multiplex networks¶

In this section we concentrate on the evolution of sandpile model on a multiplex network. Essentially, a multiplex network is a network with more than one layer, and in each layer we have the same nodes but possibly with different links.

In Lee et al. (2012) [5] they investigated the evolution of a sandpile model on a multiplex network made of two layers, in which each layer can interact with the other, and studied (both analytically and numerically) how does the behaviour of the system changes when the two layers are coupled with respect to when they evolve independently. In particular, the authors studied three different situations: two random network layers, two scale-free layers (with $\gamma = 2.5$), and finally the coupling between one random layer and one scale free layer. In this section we will perform similar simulations, trying to replicate and further analyze their results.

The coupled evolution of the two layers of the multiple network, as it was implemented in [5], can be described as follows:

  1. Each node has a threshold equal to its degree in each layer, so the threshold of a node can be different in the two layers
  2. At each time step, a node and a layer are chosen randomly, and a grain is given to that node in that layer. So even the number of grains on each node are different and separated for the two layers
  3. If, in any layer, the number of grains on one node is equal or higher than the threshold of that node in that layer, the node topples (from now on, we will refer as the unstable layer to the layer where the threshold has been exceeded)
  4. An unstable node topples in both layers: in the unstable layer it gives one grain to each neighbour, while in the other layer it loses all the grains it has and gives them to a random subsample of its neighbours
  5. If these topplings create other unstable nodes, those nodes topple as described in the previous point

In our simulations, we will use a small simplification: at each time step, only the nodes toppling in the layer where the grain was added can cause topplings in the other layer. So, at each time step we have a main layer (the one where the grain is added), which is the only one that can trigger topplings in the other layer: if the induced toppling in the non-main layer causes an avalanche, that does not influence the main layer. With this simplification the simulation of the evolution of the system is much simpler, but we will see that it still leads to interesting results.

5.1 Results¶

First, we analyze the sandpile model on a multiplex network where each layer is a regular lattice of side $N=100$. To simulate the evolution of the system, we can use the SandNet.Multiplex class, a container of two or more SandNet.Model objects that allows the co-evolution of the different models as explained before.

For all the simulations we will do from now on using multiplex structures with two layers, we will run the simulation for 60000 time steps. All the simulations before have been done with 30000 time steps, but here grains are added only on one layer at each time step, so we need to double the time of evolution to get results comparable with the previous ones.

To avoid redundancy, here we will only put the plots of number of topplings per node, which carry the most relevant results. The plot of avalanche size and avalanche area distributions, with the fit to a powerlaw, can be visualized in the right folders.

In [15]:
#create and evolve the multiplex network
original_model_1 = SandNet.Model(N = 100, initial_grains = 'random', seed = 42)
original_model_2 = SandNet.Model(N = 100, initial_grains = 'random', seed = 43)
double_original_multiplex = SandNet.Multiplex([original_model_1, original_model_2])
double_original_multiplex.evolve_together(60000, together = True, seed = 42)

#plot avalanche size distribution and fit to a powerlaw
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(original_model_1, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution square grid 1", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_1, figure_1 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(original_model_1, figure = figure_1, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(original_model_1, figure = figure_1, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(original_model_2, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution square grid 2", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_2, figure_2 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(original_model_2, figure = figure_2, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(original_model_2, figure = figure_2, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_square_grid/avalanche_size_square_grid_1.png")
figure_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_square_grid/avalanche_size_square_grid_2.png")

print("Avalanche size exponent network 1: " + str(exponent_1))
print("Avalanche size exponent network 2: " + str(exponent_2))
plt.close("all")

#plot number of topplings per node
topplings_per_node_1 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(original_model_1, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node square grid 1", cmap = "viridis",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
topplings_per_node_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_square_grid/topplings_per_node_square_grid_1.png")

topplings_per_node_2 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(original_model_2, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node square grid 2", cmap = "viridis",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
topplings_per_node_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_square_grid/topplings_per_node_square_grid_2.png")

#delete variables
del double_original_multiplex, original_model_1, original_model_2
garbage_collector = gc.collect()

Avalanche size exponent network 1: 0.3135985656704868
Avalanche size exponent network 2: 0.2822297396071481

The avalanche size exponents obtained, as well as the number of topplings per node, clearly show that evolving a sandpile model on a single square grid or on two coupled square grids does not make any difference. So, we move to the simulation of sandpile model on two random network layers, where results start becoming more interesting.

We use again the same method used before to build random and scale-free networks, always with $10^4$ nodes and the parameter $m = 2$, which means a mean node degree of 4.

In [16]:
#create and evolve the multiplex network
random_graph_1 = build_network(10000, 2, 0, seed = 42)
random_graph_2 = build_network(10000, 2, 0, seed = 43)

#plot avalanche size distribution and fit to a powerlaw
random_model_1 = SandNet.Model(random_graph_1, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 42)
random_model_2 = SandNet.Model(random_graph_2, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 43)
double_random_multiplex = SandNet.Multiplex([random_model_1, random_model_2])
double_random_multiplex.evolve_together(60000, together = True, lose_probability = 0.001, seed = 42)

figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(random_model_1, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution random network 1", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_1, figure_1 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(random_model_1, figure = figure_1, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(random_model_1, figure = figure_1, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(random_model_2, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution random network 2", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_2, figure_2 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(random_model_2, figure = figure_2, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(random_model_2, figure = figure_2, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_random/avalanche_size_random_network_1.png")
figure_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_random/avalanche_size_random_network_2.png")

print("Avalanche size exponent network 1: " + str(exponent_1))
print("Avalanche size exponent network 2: " + str(exponent_2))
plt.close("all")

#plot number of topplings per node
topplings_per_node_1 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(random_model_1, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node random network 1", cmap = "plasma",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
topplings_per_node_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_random/topplings_per_node_random_network_1.png")
topplings_per_node_2 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(random_model_2, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node random network 2", cmap = "plasma",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
topplings_per_node_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_random/topplings_per_node_random_network_2.png")

#delete variables
del double_random_multiplex, random_model_1, random_model_2, random_graph_1, random_graph_2
garbage_collector = gc.collect()

Avalanche size exponent network 1: 0.4270871583659853
Avalanche size exponent network 2: 0.4134140482246184

We immediately notice how the avalanche size distribution is still well fitted by a power law, meaning that the system is still able to reach criticality. However, the coupling of the two network decreases significantly the avalanche size exponent in both layers: we have $\tau = 1.43$ in the first layer and $\tau = 1.41$ in the second layer, against the value of $1.52$ for the sandpile on a single random network. This means that we have a deviation from the mean field behaviour, and that the probability to have a larger avalanche is (slightly) higher.

Moreover, the shape of the number of topplings distribution has completely changed with respect to the model on single network. Now it looks like topplings are more concentrated on higher degree nodes, and especially nodes with degree 1 topple a significantly lower amount of times. This behaviour can be probably explained considering that nodes of degree equal to 1 have a threshold of 1, which means that when they are stable they have 0 grains. So, when the corresponding node on the other layer topples, they cannot be induced to topple, because they have no grains to give their neighbour.

We finally simulate the sandpile model on two scale-free network layers. In [5] they used an exponent $\gamma = 2.5$ to build the scale-free network layers, but here we use $\gamma = 2.6$, since this value of $\gamma$ have been already used for one of thee simulations in Section 4.3, and so results can be compared more easily.

In [17]:
#create and evolve the multiplex network
alpha = 1/1.6
scale_free_graph_1 = build_network(10000, 2, alpha, seed = 42)
scale_free_graph_2 = build_network(10000, 2, alpha, seed = 43)
scale_free_model_1 = SandNet.Model(scale_free_graph_1, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 42)
scale_free_model_2 = SandNet.Model(scale_free_graph_2, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 43)
double_scale_free_multiplex = SandNet.Multiplex([scale_free_model_1, scale_free_model_2])
double_scale_free_multiplex.evolve_together(60000, together = True, lose_probability = 0.001, seed = 42)

#plot avalanche size distribution and fit to a powerlaw
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(scale_free_model_1, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution scale free network 1", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_1, figure_1 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(scale_free_model_1, figure = figure_1, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(scale_free_model_1, figure = figure_1, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(scale_free_model_2, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution scale free network 2", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_2, figure_2 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(scale_free_model_2, figure = figure_2, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(scale_free_model_2, figure = figure_2, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_scale_free/avalanche_distribution_scale_free_network_1.png")
figure_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_scale_free/avalanche_distribution_scale_free_network_2.png")

print("Avalanche size exponent network 1: " + str(exponent_1))
print("Avalanche size exponent network 2: " + str(exponent_2))
plt.close("all")

#plot number of topplings per node
topplings_per_node_1 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(scale_free_model_1, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node network 1", cmap = "gist_rainbow",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5, norm = LogNorm())
topplings_per_node_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_scale_free/topplings_per_node_scale_free_1.png")

topplings_per_node_2 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(scale_free_model_2, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node network 2", cmap = "gist_rainbow",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5, norm = LogNorm())
topplings_per_node_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/double_scale_free/topplings_per_node_scale_free_2.png")

#delete variables
del double_scale_free_multiplex, scale_free_model_2, scale_free_graph_2
garbage_collector = gc.collect()

Avalanche size exponent network 1: 0.7269907820542627
Avalanche size exponent network 2: 0.638671618708916

The results are very similar to what we obtained for the double random network: the avalanche size exponent is decreased (with respect to the value $\tau = 1.80$ obtained for the single scale free network with the same exponent), and nodes with degree equal to 1 have a smaller probability to topple. In this case, however, we can also notice a significant difference between the first and the second layer: the two avalanche size exponents are significantly different, respectively $\tau_1 = 1.73$ and $\tau_2 = 1.64$, and the difference between the number of topplings of the one-degree nodes and the other nodes is highly evident only in the first layer. We will not try to give an explanation to this difference, but it probably just involves how the two layers are randomly built, as well as the correspondences between the nodes in the two layers.

We can also try to simulate the sandpile model on a multiplex network with one random and one scale free layer, but the results obtained are not much different from those already shown for a double random network and a double scale free network. The only relevant difference is a value of the avalanche size exponent for the scale free layer of $\tau = 1.58$, even smaller than those obtained for the double scale free network. So, to avoid redundancy, we put all the plots for the mixed multiplex network in this folder, while the code to generate these plots and to obtain the exponents is left as an Appendix.

6. Conclusions¶

In this project we saw some of the main results concerning sandpile model on complex network structures. We mainly focused on reviewing two papers ([3] and [5]), but we re-implemented their simulations, also with some small modifications, and then we did our own independent analysis.

We first analyzed the original sandpile model on a square grid, using first numerical simulations and then presenting a mean field approach to describe analytically the evolution. We saw that the two methods give different results for the avalanche size exponent, since the mean field theory is not capable of capturing the spatial correlations that characterize a regular lattice structure.

Then we studied the sandpile model on two type of networks: random networks and scale free networks. We saw how the lack of a regular structure in a random network makes the mean field approximation correct, and the avalanche size exponent obtained through simulations is equal to the one derived analytically. For scale free networks, we showed that, while for high values of the scale free exponent $\gamma$ the behaviour is similar to that of a random network, as $\gamma \rightarrow 2$ the avalanche size exponent increases, deviating from the mean field behaviour. However, at the same time the number of avalanches of larger size increases with respect to avalanche of smaller size, making the avalanche size distribution deviating from the power law behaviour, and leaving it unclear whether the system has reached a critical state or not.

Finally, we analyzed the sandpile model on a multiplex network, and in particular on networks of two layers where the topplings on one layer could influence also the other layer. We noticed in particular that, when coupling together in a multiplex structure two random or two scale free networks, the avalanche size exponents tend to decrease in both layer with respect to the case of a one-layer network. Moreover, the contribution of nodes with different degrees to the total number of topplings changes completely in a multiplex network: the interaction between the two layers makes lower degree nodes less likely to topple.

There are many other works that have been done, and that probably will be done, on SOC and sandpile model on complex network structures: in this project we only saw a couple of them. What can be said for sure is that the evolution of the sandpile model, as well as the reaching of a self-organized critical state, is deeply influenced by the network structure on which the model evolves.

Appendix¶

In this Appendix we leave the code used to simulate the evolution of a sandpile model on a multiplex network with one random layer and one scale free layer. The code is analog to that already used for all the other simulations on multiplex networks, and it generates all the plots that can be seen in this folder, and gives the avalanche size exponents of $\tau = 1.58$ for the scale free layer and $\tau = 1.46$ for the random layer.

In [18]:
#create and evolve the multiplex network
alpha = 1/1.6
scale_free_graph_1 = build_network(10000, 2, alpha, seed = 42)
random_graph_2 = build_network(10000, 2, 0, seed = 42)

scale_free_model_1 = SandNet.Model(scale_free_graph_1, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 42)
random_model_2 = SandNet.Model(random_graph_2, threshold_rule='degree', initial_grains = 'random', seed = 43)
mixed_multiplex = SandNet.Multiplex([scale_free_model_1, random_model_2])
mixed_multiplex.evolve_together(60000, together = True, lose_probability = 0.001, seed = 42)

#plot avalanche size distribution and fit to a powerlaw
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(scale_free_model_1, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution network 1 (scale free)", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_1, figure_1 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(scale_free_model_1, figure = figure_1, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_1 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(scale_free_model_1, figure = figure_1, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_size(random_model_2, x_label = "Avalanche size", y_label = "Density",
                                     title = "Avalanche distribution network 2 (random)", label = "measured avalanche sizes")
exponent_2, figure_2 = SandNet.fit_powerlaw(random_model_2, figure = figure_2, color = "red", linestyle = "--", label = "fit avalanche sizes")
figure_2 = SandNet.plot_avalanche_area(random_model_2, figure = figure_2, color="orange", label = "measured avalanche areas")

figure_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/mixed/avalanche_distribution_scale_free_1.png")
figure_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/mixed/avalanche_distribution_random_2.png")

print("Avalanche size exponent network 1 (scale free): " + str(exponent_1))
print("Avalanche size exponent network 2 (random): " + str(exponent_2))
plt.close("all")

#plot number of topplings per node
topplings_per_node_1 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(scale_free_model_1, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node network 1 (scale free)", cmap = "gist_rainbow",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5, norm = LogNorm())
topplings_per_node_1.savefig("./plots/multiplex/mixed/topplings_per_node_scale_free_1.png")

topplings_per_node_2 = SandNet.plot_topplings_per_node(random_model_2, x_label = "Nodes", y_label = "Number of topplings (log)",
                                                          title = "Number of topplings per node network 2 (random)", cmap = "plasma",
                                                          marker = ".", linewidths = 0.5)
topplings_per_node_2.savefig("./plots/multiplex/mixed/topplings_per_node_random_2.png")
plt.close("all")

#delete variables
del alpha, mixed_multiplex, figure_1, figure_2, scale_free_model_1, scale_free_graph_1, random_model_2, random_graph_2
del topplings_per_node_1, topplings_per_node_2
garbage_collector = gc.collect()

Avalanche size exponent network 1 (scale free): 0.5801084873021597
Avalanche size exponent network 2 (random): 0.46062181377388917

References¶

[1] Per Bak, Chao Tang, and Kurt Wiesenfeld, Self-organized criticality: An explanation of the 1/f noise, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 381 (1987)

[2] Zapperi, Stefano & Lauritsen, Kent & Stanley, H.. (1995). Self-Organized Branching Processes: A Mean-Field Theory for Avalanches. Physical Review Letters. 75. 10.1103/PhysRevLett.75.4071.

[3] Goh K.-I., Lee D.-S., Kahng B., Kim D., 2003, Sandpile on Scale-Free Networks, Physical Review Letters, 91

[4] Goh K.-I., Kahng B., Kim D., 2001, Universal Behavior of Load Distribution in Scale-Free Networks, Physical Review Letters, 87

[5] Lee K.-M., Goh K. I., Kim I. M., 2012, Sandpiles on multiplex networks, Journal of the Korean Physical Society, 60, 641–647
